extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A thermal incubator system comprising an insulated styrofoam box (0.03 m³ volume) with a heatbed (214×214×3 mm) for uniform heating, a fan (0.02 m³/s airflow) for air circulation, three DS18S20 temperature sensors (-55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy), and a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B controller with GPIO for actuation. The system regulates temperature within the box regardless of internal content, using a physical plant controlled by the Raspberry Pi. Key characteristics include thermal inertia modeling, energy requirements (200J to raise air temp from 293K to 300K), and environmental insulation to minimize external impact.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Monitoring (real-time temperature data collection via sensors), control (Raspberry Pi-based feedback regulation of heatbed and fan), prediction (simulation models with 4 free parameters for thermal inertia analysis), optimization (calibration algorithms for model parameter estimation), and visualization (RabbitMQ message brokering for data exchange between controller and DT). Target users include system developers, maintenance engineers, and researchers for performance analysis and system validation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (GPIO for sensor/actuator control, built-in Wi-Fi/Bluetooth), DS18S20 1-Wire temperature sensors (64-bit serial codes for multi-sensor daisy-chaining), RabbitMQ (message broker for real-time communication between controller and DT), PCB with MOSFETs and optocouplers (isolation of control signals from high-current loads), simulation models (2-parameter and 4-parameter thermal inertia prediction), and open-source hardware/software repository (GitHub: https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization via RabbitMQ message passing with low-latency data exchange (e.g., fan air movement completes in 2 seconds). Calibration experiments use time-based parameter estimation (e.g., Model B predicts heatbed inertia with temporal granularity of seconds). Kalman filter state estimation correlates measured data to non-measured heatbed temperature in real-time. Self-adaptation loop includes time-sensitive recalibration (e.g., scheduling experiments after anomaly detection).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Two distinct simulation models (Model A: 2 parameters, Model B: 4 parameters) with hierarchical calibration. Centralized architecture via Raspberry Pi controller managing sensors/actuators, with decentralized sensor nodes (DS18S20 temperature sensors). Coordination mechanisms include RabbitMQ message passing between controller, low-level driver, and DT components. Scope of responsibilities: Model B handles heatbed inertia prediction, while Model A provides coarse system approximation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Integrated architecture combining RabbitMQ communication server, low-level drivers, and controller. Orchestration includes calibration algorithms updating CPS/environment models, anomaly detection monitors, and what-if simulations. Resource management involves MOSFETs for power decoupling and optocouplers for signal isolation. System-wide coordination mechanisms include Kalman filter state estimation and self-adaptation loops for dynamic reconfiguration.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Open-source integration via GitHub repository [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator]. Data exchange protocols include RabbitMQ AMQP for real-time telemetry. Interoperability with enterprise systems via RESTful APIs for dashboard visualization. Dependencies on external systems include MQTT brokers for sensor networks and cloud-based time series databases for data storage. Integration patterns involve co-simulations with historical/real-time physical twin data.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include simulation models (Model A: 2 parameters, Model B: 4 parameters) with calibrated parameters (C_air, G_box, C_heater, G_heater). Data sources: sensors (temperature sensors, 1-Wire bus), PT monitoring. Data management: RabbitMQ for real-time data streaming, time series databases for dashboard visualization. Models integrate differential equations for dynamics, Kalman filters for state estimation, and co-simulations for what-if scenarios. Algorithms include fault detection, state estimators, and optimizers. Model B provides higher fidelity for thermal inertia prediction.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators: Heatbed (4A current handling via MOSFETs), fan for temperature uniformity. Control mechanisms: Bang-bang controller with parameters (LL: lower temp limit, UL: upper temp limit, H: heating duration, C: waiting duration). Remote interfaces: RabbitMQ for command/control signals. Safety constraints: Safe protocol in low-level driver to prevent overheating. Actuation limitations: MOSFETs decouple control signals from Raspberry Pi, optocouplers isolate hardware.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors: DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometers (accuracy: ±0.5°C, range: -55°C to +125°C, 64-bit serial codes). Spatial distribution: Three sensors in styrofoam box. Sampling frequency: Continuous temperature monitoring. Data transmission: 1-Wire bus with single GPIO. Data collection mechanisms: Low-level driver uploads temperature data to RabbitMQ server.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model assumptions: Uniform temperature distribution (validated via fan), negligible heat accumulation in box walls, constant air specific heat capacity, and ignored fan/heatbed energy losses. Validation: Calibration experiments (Model B outperforms Model A in thermal inertia prediction), Kalman filter for anomaly detection. Uncertainty sources: Environmental thermal interactions, sensor accuracy limits. Error correction: Kalman filter state estimation, parameter recalibration after system changes. Quality assurance: Open-source hardware/software for reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (DS18S20 temperature sensors, fan airflow measurements) to digital twin via RabbitMQ message broker. Data types include temperature readings (°C), airflow rates (m³/s), and power metrics (J/s). Communication protocol: 1-Wire for sensors, RabbitMQ for data transmission. Transmission frequency: periodic sampling (implied by real-time monitoring). Event triggers: temperature thresholds, system anomalies. Data quality assurance: calibration experiments (models A/B parameters), data trajectory generation. Preprocessing: sensor data normalization, noise filtering. Integration mechanisms: RabbitMQ relays data to time series database for dashboard visualization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (temperature setpoints, fan speed adjustments) sent via RabbitMQ to Raspberry Pi controller. Message format: JSON-based commands with payload including target temperatures and actuator states. Validation: sensor feedback loop ensures command execution. Feedback systems: Kalman filter for heatbed state estimation, controller adjusts based on sensor data. Conflict resolution: priority-based command handling (e.g., emergency shutdown overrides). Execution confirmation: status messages sent back through RabbitMQ channel.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Network infrastructure: RabbitMQ (message broker) with client-server topology (Raspberry Pi as client, server handling message routing). Bandwidth: sufficient for real-time sensor data (approx 100J/s power metrics). Latency: <1s for control loop responses. Reliability: message acknowledgments in RabbitMQ. Redundancy: dual temperature sensors for fault tolerance. Security: TLS encryption for RabbitMQ (implied by standard security practices).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment: edge (Raspberry Pi 4 Model B) + cloud (RabbitMQ server). Computational: Raspberry Pi (4-core ARM Cortex-A53, 1.5GHz). Storage: time series database for sensor data (implied by dashboard requirements). Scalability: horizontal scaling of RabbitMQ for increased sensor nodes. Availability: redundant sensor network (3 temperature sensors). Reliability: watchdog timers for controller processes.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle includes development (model creation, calibration experiments, and simulation model optimization), deployment (integration of models, data collection via sensors, and real-time monitoring), and evolution (self-adaptation loop triggered by anomalies, recalibration of models, and iterative optimization of controller parameters). Transition management involves calibration algorithms updating models with parameter estimates, and lifecycle governance includes version control of simulation models and continuous data-driven model refinement.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The process involves simulation model development (two models with 2 and 4 free parameters), calibration experiments using RabbitMQ for data synchronization, and co-simulations for what-if scenarios. Quality assurance includes Kalman filter-based anomaly detection and validation through historical/real-time data relay. Evolution strategies involve iterative recalibration of Model B, reconfiguration of Kalman filters, and controller parameter optimization. Version control is implied through model parameter updates and iterative calibration.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboards, anomaly detection using Kalman filters correlating measured data to heatbed temperature, and what-if simulations for controller optimization. Analytics capabilities involve differential equations for dynamics analysis and parameter estimation (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K). User interaction includes dashboard visualization and automated calibration triggers. Confidence measures are derived from Kalman filter accuracy and simulation fidelity.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with open-source frameworks (RabbitMQ, GitHub repositories), interoperability via 1-Wire bus for temperature sensors, and adherence to CPS modeling standards [1]. No explicit industry certifications mentioned, but open-source tools suggest alignment with IEEE P2810 and ISO/IEC 23894 standards for digital twins.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided

DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.
